20: qorov lanu ha-ish, mige-aleinu hu: 'The man is a close relative of ours, one of our redeemers.' The go'el (kinsman-redeemer) is a legal institution in Israelite law: a near male relative who has the right and duty to redeem a family member's sold land (Leviticus 25:25), to marry a brother's childless widow (Deuteronomy 25:5-10, levirate marriage), to redeem a relative sold into slavery (Leviticus 25:47-55), and to avenge the blood of a murdered kinsman (the go'el hadam). Boaz fulfills the first two functions. Paul in Galatians uses the redemption/buying-back vocabulary (exagorazo, 'redeemed from the curse of the law', Galatians 3:13; Ruth 4:5) to describe Christ's work — the kinsman-redeemer framework is the legal-theological background for NT redemption language.
2: Ruth proposes to ʾălaqqəṭāh — to glean (lāqaṭ) in the fields. Gleaning is a formal legal institution in the Pentateuch: Leviticus 19:9-10 and 23:22 mandate that harvesters must not reap the corners of the field or go back over fallen grain, leaving it for the poor and the sojourner (gēr). Ruth is both poor and a foreign sojourner — she qualifies in both categories. Her phrase 'after one in whose eyes I may find favor' (ʾāḥar ʾăšer emṣāʾ ḥēn bəʿênāyw) uses ḥēn (grace/favor), the companion term to ḥeseḏ: ḥēn is the favor shown by a superior to an inferior; ḥeseḏ is the loyalty shown between covenant parties. Ruth will receive both from Boaz.
3: 'And her chance chanced upon' — wayyiqer miqrehāh — is the narrator's deliberate irony: the same root (qārāh, to happen/chance) doubled to signal that what appears accidental is providential. The identical construction appears in Genesis 24:12 where Abraham's servant prays to 'meet' (haqreh nāʾ lᵉpānay) the right woman for Isaac — that too is a divinely orchestrated 'chance' meeting. The narrator refuses to say 'YHWH sent her to Boaz's field'; instead the reader is invited to see the divine hand behind human coincidences too apt to be coincidental. This is the book of Ruth's theological method: YHWH is never directly said to act in chapters 2-3, yet the entire plot moves through human contingencies that YHWH is clearly directing.
12: Boaz's blessing over Ruth is a theological hinge: yiššalem YHWH pāʿolāṯēḵ... ʾăšer bāʾt laḥǎsôt taḥat kənāpāyw — 'May YHWH repay your work... under whose wings you have come to take refuge.' The keyword kānāp (כָּנָף) — wing, but also skirt/garment corner — appears here for the first time. YHWH's protective kānāp is the image of divine shelter (Psalms 91:4: 'he will cover you with his pinions, under his wings you will find refuge'). This same word reappears in 3:9 when Ruth asks Boaz to 'spread your kānāp over your servant' — making explicit that she is asking Boaz to be what he prayed YHWH would be. The kānāp connection is the book's central typological hinge.
1: ulnaomi meyuda levishah ish gibbor chayil mishpachat elimelech ushmo boaz: 'Now Naomi had a kinsman on her husband's side (meyuda, a known one), a man of standing (gibbor chayil) from the family of Elimelech. His name was Boaz.' The narrator introduces Boaz before Ruth meets him. Gibbor chayil (man of standing/valor/wealth) is the standard term for a man of means and social standing — the same phrase applied to Gideon (Judges 6:12), Jephthah (Judges 11:1), and later Kish (1 Samuel 9:1). When Boaz later uses the feminine form (eshet chayil, woman of worth, Ruth 3:11) of Ruth, the verbal echo connects them: he is a man of worth, she is a woman of worth — a matched pair. The name Boaz may mean 'in him is strength' — a fitting name for the one who will act as goel.
4: veyihineh boaz ba mibeit lechem vayyomer laqotzerim YHWH imachem: 'Just then Boaz arrived from Bethlehem and greeted the reapers: “YHWH be with you.” And they said: “YHWH bless you.”' Boaz's entrance is marked by the same providence-word hinneh (look! behold!) that marks divine appearances and significant arrivals throughout the OT. His greeting — YHWH imachem (YHWH be with you) — and the reapers' response — yivarechcha YHWH (YHWH bless you) — establish the field as a space of covenant blessing. Boaz speaks YHWH's name twice in greeting before any action occurs. This is not incidental piety but characterization: this is a man whose daily speech is saturated with covenant awareness.
5: vayyomer boaz lena'aro hannitzav al-haqotzerim lemi hana'arah hazot: 'Boaz asked his servant who was in charge of the reapers: “Whose young woman is this?”' The question lemi hana'arah (to whom does this young woman belong?) reflects the social structure: a woman gleaning alone was typically attached to a household. Boaz's noticing of Ruth begins the plot's central movement. The na'ar (servant/lad) in charge of the reapers — an overseer — will convey Boaz's instructions and Boaz's character to the reader through his report about Ruth (v6-7).
6: vaya'an hana'ar hannitzav al-haqotzerim vayyomer na'arah mo'aviyah hi hashavah im naomi misedei moav: 'The foreman answered: “She is the Moabite young woman who returned with Naomi from Moab.”' The first thing said about Ruth to Boaz is her Moabite identity. The foreman's accurate identification — she is na'arah mo'aviyah (the Moabite young woman) — is the social and ethnic fact that makes Ruth's subsequent treatment by Boaz all the more remarkable. Boaz will show favor to someone his culture had reason to exclude. The ethnic designation is not the narrator's prejudice but a setup for the reversal of expected social behavior.
7: vatomer elaqqetah-na veasafti va'omerim achar haqotzerim: 'She asked: “Please let me glean and gather among the sheaves after the reapers.”' Ruth's request uses the particle of entreaty na (please) — she is asking permission, not asserting a right. Her request goes beyond the standard gleaning (picking up what falls) to gleaning 'among the sheaves' (ve-asafti va-omerim) — a more aggressive form. The narrator adds: vatavo vata'amod meaz haboker ve'ad attah zeh shivtah habayit me'at — 'she came, and has been standing from early morning until now, except that she sat in the house a little.' Ruth's diligence is established: she arrived early, worked continuously, and her only break was minimal rest. Her work ethic reinforces her character.
8: halo shamaat biti al-telchi liqlot besadeh acher: 'Listen carefully, my daughter. Do not go to glean in another field.' Boaz's address biti (my daughter) establishes a protective, familial register — he is an older kinsman speaking with paternal authority and warmth, not merely an employer to an employee. His prohibition 'do not go to another field' is a guarantee of provision: he is taking on responsibility for her welfare. The phrase vekhen tigdavein el-na'arotai (stay close to my young women) provides the mechanism: Ruth is to be integrated into Boaz's household workforce, no longer gleaning as an independent poor woman but as someone under his protection.
9: einayich basadeh asher-yiqtzoru vehalachti achareihen: 'Watch the field where they are reaping and follow along after them.' Boaz provides Ruth a practical strategy for safe gleaning: staying in the visible, supervised area of his field, following his own workers. halo tzivieti et-hane'arim levilthi nege'a bach: 'I have commanded the young men not to touch you.' The prohibition here is against naga (to touch/harm) — Boaz explicitly protects Ruth from male harassment, a real danger for a lone foreign woman gleaning. His protection addresses a vulnerability Ruth herself named to Naomi later (v22: stay with his workers so 'they do not fall upon you in another field'). vetzamit valacht el-hakkeilim veshatit mimme asher yishavun hana'arim: 'and when you are thirsty, go to the vessels and drink what the young men have drawn.' Even water — normally a significant provision in a dry-season harvest — Boaz provides proactively.
10: vatippol al-paneiha vatishtachu artzah vatomer elav madua matzati chen be'einecha lehakkireini ve'anochi nokhriyvah: 'She fell with her face to the ground and bowed, and said to him: “Why have I found favor (chen) in your sight, to the point of noticing me — and I am a foreigner (nokhriyvah)?”' Ruth's prostration is the full covenant-reverence gesture. Her rhetorical question expresses genuine astonishment: chen (favor, grace) is not what a foreign gleaner expected. The word nokhriyvah (foreigner, stranger) — more distant than ger (sojourner) — marks Ruth as culturally outside the covenant community. Her own self-designation heightens the contrast with Boaz's generosity. He will shortly resolve the tension between her foreign status and his response to her: her chesed to Naomi is the reason (v11).
11: vayya'an boaz vayyomer lah hugad hugad li kol asher asit et chamotekh: 'Boaz answered her: “All that you have done for your mother-in-law has been fully reported to me.”' The infinitive absolute hugad hugad (lit. 'it has been told, told' = it has been fully reported) emphasizes the completeness of his information. Boaz knows everything: the abandonment of her home country and her father's household, and the commitment to a mother-in-law in her grief. va-telchi el-am asher lo-yada'at tmol shilshom: 'and that you have come to a people you did not know before.' The phrase lo-yada'at tmol shilshom (you did not know before) underscores the radical nature of Ruth's commitment: she came to a complete stranger people. This is precisely the Abrahamic paradigm — leaving the known for the covenant people (Genesis 12:1).
13: vatomer emtzah-chen be'einecha adoni ki nichhamtani vechi dibbarta al-lev shiphchatecha: 'She said: “May I continue to find favor in your sight, my lord — for you have comforted me (nichhamtani) and spoken to the heart (dibbarta al-lev) of your maidservant.”' Nichham (to comfort) is the verb of prophetic comfort: YHWH comforts his people (nachem nachem ammi, Isaiah 40:1). Ruth's use of this word for Boaz's treatment of her builds the latent typology: Boaz as image of YHWH's comforting concern for the outcast and bereaved. Dibbarta al-lev (speak to the heart) is an idiom of deep emotional reassurance (Genesis 34:3; Ruth 50:21; Judges 19:3; Isaiah 40:2; Hosea 2:16). ve-anochi lo ehyeh kechachat shifchotecha: 'although I am not even as one of your maidservants' — Ruth's continued self-diminishment in the face of Boaz's kindness, which she regards as exceeding her station.
14: vayyomer lah boaz le'et ha-ochel geshi halom ve'achalt min-halechem: 'At mealtime Boaz said to her: “Come here and eat some bread.”' The invitation to eat at Boaz's table is social inclusion: Ruth is invited to the inner circle of workers who eat with the owner. vatetaleh hemtzaltah vatochal vatisba vattothar: 'she dipped her morsel in the vinegar, and sat beside the reapers, and he passed to her parched grain (qali), and she ate until she was satisfied (vatisba) and had some left over (vatothar).' The progression — satiation and surplus — answers Naomi's self-description in v21: 'YHWH has brought me back empty (reqam).' Naomi said 'empty'; the narrator shows Ruth eating to satiation and having leftovers to bring home. The reversal begins with Boaz's table.
15: vataqam lilaqqet vayetzav boaz et-ne'arav lemor gam bein ha-omerim telaqqet: 'When she rose to glean again, Boaz commanded his young men: “Let her glean even among the sheaves.”' Boaz's command goes beyond his initial permission: she may glean bein ha-omerim (among the sheaves), not merely in the perimeter where fallen grain lies. This is direct intervention on her behalf, exceeding legal obligation. The gleaning laws required leaving the edges and fallen grain; Boaz gives Ruth access to the harvested portion itself. velo techferu otah: 'and do not rebuke her' — the young men are explicitly told not to embarrass or shame her for gleaning in the harvested area. Boaz removes every obstacle to Ruth's provision.
16: vegam shol tashollu lah min hatzevutim: 'pull out some stalks from the bundles and drop them deliberately for her.' Shol tashollu (pull out, you shall pull out) — another infinitive absolute for emphasis. The instruction is to create artificial abundance for Ruth: the workers are to create 'falls' that look accidental but are engineered by Boaz's command. This is provision beyond gleaning law, done covertly — Ruth will think her gleaning is productive when in fact the abundance is Boaz's gift. The hiddenness of the grace is part of its character: Boaz does not make Ruth dependent on public charity but dignifies her labor while covertly multiplying its fruit. This is a model for chesed: generous beyond obligation, protecting the dignity of the recipient.
17: vatilaqqet basadeh ad ha'erev vatachbot et asher liqqetah vayehi ke'efah se'orim: 'So she gleaned in the field until evening. When she beat out what she had gleaned, it came to about an ephah of barley.' An ephah of barley is approximately 22 liters — far more than a typical day's gleaning. The surplus is the fruit of Boaz's hidden providence (v16). The vast quantity will strike Naomi as unusual (v19: 'Where did you glean today?') and trigger the revelation of Boaz's identity. Abundance is the sign by which Naomi will recognize YHWH's hand through his human agent.
18: vatissa vatavo hair vatare chamotah et asher liqqetah: 'She took it up and went into the town. Her mother-in-law saw what she had gleaned.' The verb vatissa (she lifted up, carried) describes Ruth carrying the substantial ephah of grain into Bethlehem — a visible sign of the day's provision. The domestic scene: Ruth shows Naomi what she gleaned, then gives her the leftover meal from Boaz's table. The detail that Ruth 'also gave her what she had left over after being satisfied' (vatotzieh vatitenen lah et asher hotarah miseba'ah) doubles the provision: not only an ephah of grain but also the portion from Boaz's own meal. The day of gleaning has become a day of abundance.
19: vatomer lah chamotah eifo liqqatat hayom ve'anah asit baruch makkirech: 'Her mother-in-law asked her: “Where did you glean today? Where did you work? Blessed be the one who noticed you!”' Naomi's question follows the surplus logic: the quantity of grain demands an explanation. Baruch makkirech (blessed be the one who took notice of you) is a blessing spoken before the name is known — Naomi intuitively recognizes divine providence in the abundance before she knows its instrument. The blessing is fulfilled when Boaz speaks the same baruk form over Ruth (Ruth 3:10). The chain of blessing connects the human agents to the divine source.
21: vatomer Rut hamoa'viyah gam ki amar elay im-hane'arim asher-li tidbaqin ad im-killu et kol-haqatzir asher-li: 'Ruth the Moabitess added: “He also told me: Stay close to my workers until they have finished all my harvest.”' Ruth quotes Boaz's instruction with the Hebrew form tidebeqin (stay/cleave close) — the same root (dbq) used for Ruth's own clinging to Naomi in v14: 'she clung (davqah) to her.' Ruth's attachment to Naomi and Boaz's instruction to cleave to his workers both use the dbq covenant-loyalty word — the word of marital bond (Genesis 2:24) and covenant faithfulness applied to field-working. The entire plot is a network of dbq loyalties.
22: vatomer naomi el-Rut kallateha tov biti ki tetze'i im-na'arotav velo yifge'u bach besadeh acher: 'Naomi said to Ruth her daughter-in-law: “It is good, my daughter, that you go out with his young women, so they do not fall upon you in another field.”' Yifge'u bach (they fall upon you, meet you with hostile intent, harm you) — paga is often a violent encounter word. Naomi understands the social danger Ruth faces as a foreign woman gleaning alone. Boaz's field is protective space; another field could be dangerous. Naomi's advice is practical wisdom based on social reality, but it also functions theologically: she is directing Ruth to stay within the protective sphere of the goel, not knowing yet that Boaz is more than a field-owner.
23: vatidbeq bene'arot boaz lilaqqet ad-kelot qetzir hase'orim viqetzir hachittim vateshev et-chamotah: 'So Ruth stayed close to the young women of Boaz and gleaned through the end of the barley harvest and the wheat harvest. And she lived with her mother-in-law.' The summary verse spans the entire harvest season: barley (March-April) followed by wheat (May-June) — about seven weeks, the period between Passover and Shavuot (Pentecost). Ruth's faithful gleaning throughout the entire harvest season shows sustained loyal labor. Vateshev et-chamotah (she lived with her mother-in-law) is not merely domestic information but a measure of loyalty: through both harvests she stayed with Naomi rather than pursuing other opportunities. Her constancy matches her Ruth 1 declaration.
